David, I love lot forty-seven. She is an emerging artist, but already one of the most sought-after names here. Should I bid?
I would. The provenance is clean, the work has been authenticated and it was acquired from the studio. The valuation is solid.
But prices have soared. The market is booming, and a similar piece was sold last month for almost double. Is it too speculative?
A little. She is not blue-chip yet. So set your top bid in advance and do not chase it, the most expensive lot is not always the best buy.
Then my top bid is three hundred and thirty thousand. It has been appraised at three hundred, so the valuation supports it. I will not go higher.
Perfect. That is how a collection is built, with a clear head, not in the heat of the room. Good luck with the bid.

Maria Claudia, it is David, your art advisor, before the evening sale at the gallery. I have been through the catalogue, so here is my read on the market for you. First, the trend: prices for contemporary Brazilian work have soared this year, and the segment is now the fastest-growing part of the whole auction market. Demand is strong, so the best lots will not be cheap. Lot forty-seven is the one I would watch. It is an early painting by an emerging artist who has become extremely sought-after, and the provenance is clean: the work has been authenticated, and it was acquired directly from the studio, so there are no questions about its history. It has been appraised at around three hundred thousand dollars, and a very similar piece was sold last month for almost double the estimate. So this is a strong, blue-chip name in the making. My advice: set your top bid before you walk in, and do not chase it. The market is booming, and in a booming market it is easy to overpay. Remember, a smart purchase is one where the valuation supports the price, not just the excitement in the room. If you want a safer option, the established masterpiece in lot twelve is less speculative, but it will also be the most expensive thing on the night. Call me before seven and we will decide.

Right, let me think about this before the sale tonight. David is the expert, but the decision is mine, and I want to build this collection carefully, not emotionally. So, the facts first. The market is booming, prices have soared, and the contemporary segment is the fastest-growing one. That is exciting, but a booming market is exactly when people overpay. Lot forty-seven is the most interesting piece for me: an emerging artist, clean provenance, and the work has already been authenticated. It has been appraised at around three hundred thousand, and a similar painting was sold last month for almost double. I keep reminding myself how to talk about this properly, because my old habit is to drop the passive. I want to say the painting sold for a record price, when a collector says the painting was sold for a record price. And not the work appraised at three hundred thousand, but the work was appraised, or it has been appraised. So tonight: I set my top bid in advance, I do not chase the lot, and I check that the valuation supports the price. If lot forty-seven goes too high, I walk away. The most expensive painting in the room is not always the best buy. A good purchase is one that was made with a clear head.

First, the work leaves the artist's studio and its provenance begins: every owner and every sale is recorded. Then, before it can be sold, the piece is authenticated, so that buyers know it is genuine. Next, a specialist gives it a valuation and the auction house prints an estimate in the catalogue. After that, on the night of the sale, collectors place their bids, and the lot is sold to the highest bidder. Finally, the result is reported: the painting was sold for a record price, and the new owner is added to its provenance.
